name: Coeus
description: Coeus, also known as Polus, is a Titan in Greek mythology, representing one of the twelve children of Gaia (Earth) and Uranus (Sky). His name, though debated, is often linked to concepts of questioning, intellect, and the axis around which the heavens revolve. Coeus, unlike some of his more prominent siblings, remains a relatively obscure figure in surviving mythological narratives. He is primarily known for his role in the Titanomachy, the great war between the Titans and the Olympian gods. Despite his participation in this conflict against Zeus and the Olympians, Coeus's individual actions during the war are not explicitly detailed in most accounts. Following the Titans' defeat, Coeus, along with many of his brethren, was imprisoned in Tartarus, the deepest abyss of the underworld. His union with Phoebe produced Leto and Asteria, making him the grandfather of Apollo, Artemis, and Hecate, thereby linking him to powerful Olympian and pre-Olympian deities.
subtitle: Titan of Intellect

keyMyths.title: The Titanomachy
keyMyths.description: Coeus participated in the Titanomachy, the epic ten-year war between the Titans, led by Cronus, and the Olympian gods, led by Zeus. Coeus, alongside his brothers, fought to maintain the Titans' dominion over the cosmos. Though his specific role is not extensively detailed, his participation signifies his allegiance to the old order and his resistance to the rise of the Olympian gods. The Titans ultimately lost, and Coeus was cast into Tartarus as punishment for his rebellion. This event marks a significant turning point in Greek mythology, establishing the Olympian gods as the dominant force.

keyMyths.title: The Union with Phoebe
keyMyths.description: Coeus's most significant contribution to the mythological narrative stems from his union with Phoebe, a Titaness associated with prophecy and intellect. Their union produced two powerful daughters: Leto, the mother of Apollo and Artemis, and Asteria, who transformed into the island of Delos. This lineage connects Coeus to the Olympian pantheon and underscores the importance of his line in shaping the future of the divine world. The children of Leto and Asteria played significant roles in Greek mythology, solidifying Coeus's legacy through his descendants.

keyMyths.title: Imprisonment in Tartarus
keyMyths.description: Following the defeat of the Titans in the Titanomachy, Coeus, along with many of his brothers, was imprisoned in Tartarus, the deepest and darkest region of the underworld. This punishment, inflicted by Zeus and the Olympian gods, symbolized the complete overthrow of the old order and the establishment of Olympian authority. The Titans' imprisonment in Tartarus served as a constant reminder of the consequences of challenging the power of the new gods. The depths of Tartarus are described as a place of eternal darkness and suffering, reflecting the Titans' loss of power and status.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: A foundational text in Greek mythology, detailing the origins of the gods and the Titanomachy.
